tagline: Williamsburg's coastal Mexican obsession — Ensenada's tuna al pastor energy, Chipotle's pantry
ingredients: chicken al pastor (LTO — pineapple, achiote, chipotle)
trend_signal: al pastor avg_12m 13.0, recent_4w 13.2 rising in NY; mole avg_12m 69.8, recent_4w 72.8 rising
review_evidence: Ensenada reviewer: 'You absolutely MUST get the tuna tostadas and the pescado del día "al pastor"! Everything was awesome, but those 2 were my favorites! I could eat 7 of those tostadas and just eat the pineapple relish on the pescado with a spoon.' | Another: 'The tuna had roasted sesame, umami flavors and the scallop tostada had a zesty, citrus pop... My husband is obsessed with the crispy fish taco - says it's the best he has ever had.'
gap_rationale: Ensenada (South Williamsburg) has built a cult following around al pastor preparations — specifically the whole fish al pastor and tuna tostada al pastor. Chipotle already has al pastor chicken as an LTO. The pantry (pineapple in corn salsa, achiote in al pastor protein) supports a legitimate build.
confidence_reason: Chicken al pastor is a confirmed Chipotle LTO protein. Pineapple/achiote/chipotle are its ingredient backbone. Ensenada validates that Williamsburg diners are paying $43 for al pastor fish — Chipotle can capture the flavor at $12. Strong trend signal.

review_evidence: Ensenada reviewer: 'The steak with molé sauce... The molé sauce was underwhelming and also very salty' — even a critical review confirms mole is being ordered. Another: 'Aguachiles, moles, cocktails, and even guac all stood out as great dishes. I would recommend this place to anyone looking for actually good Mexican food in NY.'
local_evidence: Ensenada is serving mole-sauced steak as an entree and it's being ordered — even imperfect execution gets repeat mentions. Mole is the #1 trending Mexican term in NY (72.8 recent, rising, peak 100). No fast-casual operator in Williamsburg executes it. Pantry gap is enormous.
solution: Mole negro protein marinade as a dedicated SKU — braised chicken or steak in a simplified mole (ancho + chocolate + cumin + dried fruit). Does not require all 30+ traditional ingredients to be credible at fast-casual. 6-12 month development timeline. This is the single highest-opportunity flavor gap in NY.

tagline: Brooklyn's biggest Mexican trend — deeply braised, chile-rich beef that Ensenada's crowd is driving
trend_signal: birria avg_12m 37.2, recent_4w 40.0 rising in NY
review_evidence: Ensenada reviewer: 'The chips and salsa and aguachiles starters were great... this is the only place I've been to where I'm actually met with proper heat/spice.' | Another: 'Great New Mexican seafood restaurant... definitely try some tacos, made with tortillas from For All Things Good.'
gap_rationale: Birria is rising fast in NY (40.0 recent vs 37.2 avg). Chipotle's barbacoa is the closest existing protein — braised beef with chipotle and cumin already shares DNA with birria. The opportunity is positioning barbacoa in a consomme-forward build, even without true dried chile base.
confidence_reason: Barbacoa uses chipotle and cumin but not the dried guajillo/ancho that define authentic birria. The flavor direction is plausible; 'birria' branding without the consomme dipping element misses a key sensory marker. Treat as a directional build, not authentic birria.

tagline: Laser Wolf and K'Far are Williamsburg's hottest restaurants — CAVA delivers the flavor at lunch
trend_signal: sumac avg_12m 18.7, recent_4w 16.5 in NY (falling slightly but still strong); tahini avg_12m 28.5 falling but high base
review_evidence: Laser Wolf reviewer: 'The hummus was really good, super creamy and flavorful... The steak shishlik and brisket kebab were unbelievable.' | K'Far reviewer: 'The crispy mushroom hummus, very good and creamy with chunks of mushroom. For our main, we shared the mixed grill for two. Everything was excellent: cooked to perfection, flavorful, with just the right amount of spice. The meats are served on a thin pita with mango chutney.' | Another: 'The hummus, as always, was great and the food was good to excellent.'
gap_rationale: Laser Wolf and K'Far have made Israeli mezze the prestige dining format of Williamsburg — salatim spreads, lamb, hummus, tahini. CAVA's pantry is an almost perfect replica of this format at fast-casual prices. The lamb meatball + hummus + sumac slaw + eggplant + tahini build is the Laser Wolf prix fixe, deconstructed.
confidence_reason: Every ingredient is confirmed CAVA pantry. Lamb meatballs (harissa), roasted eggplant dip, hummus, sumac slaw, kalamata olives, lemon herb tahini — this is genuinely what Laser Wolf serves at $60/head. CAVA can deliver 90% of the flavor experience at $15.

tagline: Laser Wolf's shawarma-spiced cauliflower is their vegetarian star — CAVA can build it from pantry
trend_signal: cauliflower avg_12m 72.7 in NY (high base, slight fall); harissa avg_12m 11.7 falling but still relevant
review_evidence: Laser Wolf reviewer: 'Shawarma Spiced Cauliflower - my favorite of the two. The cauliflower sits on top of a creamy base of labneh. The cauliflower itself is nicely charred without getting overly soft, and is well-seasoned. The pine nuts over the top are a great finishing touch.' | K'Far reviewer: 'crispy mushroom hummus — really good... the mushrooms weren't really crispy but the hummus was still good.'
gap_rationale: Laser Wolf's shawarma-spiced cauliflower is name-dropped in multiple 5-star reviews as the vegetarian highlight — 'just as good as the meat.' CAVA's roasted vegetables (cauliflower included) + harissa + crazy feta + tahini can replicate this almost exactly.
confidence_reason: Cauliflower is in CAVA's roasted vegetables protein. Harissa, crazy feta, fiery broccoli, lemon herb tahini all confirmed pantry. Note: labneh is the one missing element from Laser Wolf's version — crazy feta is the closest available swap.

review_evidence: K'Far reviewer: 'the shakshuka was unique bc the eggs were very smoky. I liked it a lot.' | Another: 'We came here on a chilly Sunday for brunch... My mom loved the shakshuka.' | Laser Wolf reviewer on the full experience: 'their hummus is amazingly rich... the pita was warm and fresh, and the staff refilled everything when we asked.'
local_evidence: K'Far (ground floor of Hoxton hotel, Williamsburg) has made shakshuka a brunch anchor dish — it's name-checked in multiple reviews. Za'atar appears on Jerusalem bagels and across the menu. CAVA's Mediterranean identity should include both, but neither is in the pantry.
solution: Add za'atar as a dry topping SKU (low cost, no refrigeration). A warm tomato-harissa base with a soft egg topping would allow a shakshuka-style bowl — harissa base is already in pantry, eggs are the only addition needed.

tagline: Williamsburg loves Japanese-inflected bowls — Sweetgreen's miso salmon is exactly what the neighborhood wants
ingredients: nori sesame seasoning and spicy cashew
trend_signal: miso avg_12m 59.0, recent_4w 49.5 rising in NY; yuzu avg_12m 28.9, recent_4w 32.0 rising
review_evidence: Ensenada reviewer: 'The tuna had roasted sesame, umami flavors and the scallop tostada had a zesty, citrus pop.' | Kokomo reviewer on Asian-Caribbean crossover: 'Got the Crispy Chicken Bao Bun which I recommend... the XO Noodles — flavorful but not as impressive as expected.' | Laser Wolf: 'I especially enjoyed making a mini sandwich with the steak, tahini, pineapple pickle, and pita' — umami layering is what Williamsburg diners seek.
gap_rationale: Williamsburg diners at Ensenada and Kokomo show strong appetite for umami-forward, Asian-inflected preparations. Sweetgreen's Miso Glazed Salmon bowl is a confirmed menu item that lands directly in this lane. Positioning it against Ensenada's tuna tostada ($19 for 2) is viable at $16-18.
confidence_reason: Miso glazed salmon is a confirmed Sweetgreen menu item. All ingredients confirmed in pantry. Miso trend strong in NY (59.0 avg). This is a placement/emphasis play — the dish exists, the demand is local.

missing_ingredients: scotch bonnet or habanero peppers (beyond current chili use)
review_evidence: Kokomo reviewer: 'I ordered the jerk chicken wings, sweet plantains and coconut rice (some of the best any of us have had).' | Another: 'Their herbed fries were so good... I believe it's that delicious pepper sauce (just the right amount of scotch bonnet taste) they serve it with that makes them even better.' | Third: 'I had the Jerk Infused Wings, nice and saucy.' | Kokomo is mentioned in review after review for jerk chicken being a crowd anchor.
local_evidence: Kokomo (65 Kent Ave, Williamsburg) is Sweetgreen 162 N 4th's direct neighborhood competitor for the upscale-casual dinner crowd. Jerk is Kokomo's signature and the highest-trending flavor term in NY at 88.2 (peak 100). Sweetgreen has zero Caribbean pantry items.
solution: Jerk-marinated chicken as a new LTO protein SKU. Requires scotch bonnet (or habanero as substitute), allspice, thyme in the marinade — 3 SKU additions. A coconut rice base would complete the build. This is Sweetgreen's single largest flavor gap in Williamsburg.

review_evidence: Kokomo reviewer: 'I've come here an embarrassing amount of times just for that oxtail flatbread! It's my absolute favorite thing on the menu.' | Another: 'The oxtail was also really good and a personal favorite.' | Third: 'Oxtail flatbread was amazing! I was skeptical at first because I am used to traditional oxtails with rice and peas... but I loved this flatbread.'
local_evidence: Oxtail is Kokomo's #1 signature dish — mentioned in nearly every positive review. Plantain avg 22.6 (stable in NY). These are Williamsburg Caribbean staples that no fast-casual brand touches. Sweetgreen's bowl format could accommodate a braised oxtail protein.
solution: Braised oxtail protein is a significant development effort (long cook time, bone-in complication). More accessible: sweet plantain as a topping SKU (fried, sweet) paired with existing chicken or steak. Start with plantains as the quick win, build toward oxtail as a 12-month SKU project.

missing_ingredients: coconut milk or coconut rice base
review_evidence: Kokomo reviewer: 'The jerk chicken wings had a solid kick without being overpowering.' | Another: 'just the right amount of scotch bonnet taste... the pepper sauce makes them taste even better.' | Third: 'jerk infused wings, nice and saucy — we split the jerk chicken wings, braised oxtail and curry snapper with plantains and rice + beans.'
local_evidence: Jerk is the #1 trending food term in NY period (88.2 avg, peak 100, rising). Kokomo is 0.7 miles from Chipotle 130 N 4th and serves jerk as its identity dish. Chipotle has habanero in its extra hot salsa but zero jerk marinade profile — allspice and thyme are the critical missing links.
solution: Jerk-marinated chicken as a LTO protein: scotch bonnet (sourced from existing habanero supply chain), allspice, dried thyme. Serve over cilantro-lime rice with pineapple corn salsa for a Chipotle-native Caribbean-Mexican crossover. 3 pantry additions. High ROI.
